questions.question: Thwaites Glacier, that was recently seen in news, is located in which region?
questions.explanation: Scientists warned that the Thwaites Glacier is melting rapidly due to human-induced climate change. It is a massive glacier in West Antarctica, often called the Doomsday Glacier. The glacier is very wide and fast-flowing, roughly the size of Florida or Great Britain. It is extremely important because it already contributes about 4% to global annual sea-level rise. Studies show it is thinning, retreating, and adding more meltwater to the oceans. If it collapses, it could destabilise the West Antarctic Ice Sheet, causing severe sea-level rise.

questions.question: Sahyadri Tiger Reserve (STR) is located in which state?
questions.explanation: A third tigress was recently released into the wild at Sahyadri Tiger Reserve (STR), boosting tiger conservation in western Maharashtra. Sahyadri Tiger Reserve (STR) is located in the Sahyadri Ranges of the Western Ghats, Maharashtra, and is the northernmost tiger habitat in the Western Ghats. It is the first tiger reserve of Western Maharashtra and the fourth in the state.

questions.question: What is the IUCN conservation status of ‘Himalayan Griffon Vulture’ that was recently seen in news?
questions.explanation: The Himalayan Griffon Vulture was recently sighted in Melghat, Maharashtra, influenced by the presence of captive-bred vultures released in the area. It is one of the nine vulture species found in India and belongs to the Old World vultures group. It is mainly found in the Himalayan and Tibetan regions and Central Asian mountains, with limited altitudinal migration into northern India. It is a huge vulture weighing up to 12 kilograms, with long wings adapted for soaring on wind thermals. According to the International Union for Conservation of Nature (IUCN), it is listed as Near Threatened.

questions.question: What is the IUCN status of Himalayan Tahr that was recently seen in news?
questions.explanation: The Himalayan Tahr is facing a growing threat from garbage in the Himalayas. It is a large hoofed mammal with the scientific name Hemitragus jemlahicus. Its habitat spans southern Tibet, northern India, western Bhutan, and Nepal, mostly on alpine slopes between 2,500–5,000 meters. Male Himalayan Tahr weigh around 73 kg, females about 36 kg. They have a small head, pointed ears, large eyes, and backward-curving horns (males up to 46 cm, smaller in females) used in mating fights. It is listed as Near Threatened on the IUCN Red List.

questions.question: The Tamsa River, that was recently seen in news, is tributary of which river?
questions.explanation: The Tamsa River, an important tributary of the Ganga River, flows through Ambedkar Nagar, Ayodhya and Azamgarh districts of eastern Uttar Pradesh. In Azamgarh, the river was revitalised under the Namami Gange Programme through coordinated administrative action and strong community participation. A structured plan covered an 89 kilometre stretch across 111 Gram Panchayats, focusing on desilting, garbage removal, and clearing illegal encroachments. Vacant riverbank land was identified and fruit-bearing trees were planted to support ecological restoration and provide economic benefits to Gram Panchayats.

questions.question: Recently, a pair of Dusky Eagle Owls was spotted in which state after 15 years?
questions.explanation: A pair of rare dusky eagle owls was sighted in the Phato tourism zone of Terai West forest division, near Corbett Tiger Reserve, Uttarakhand, after 15 years. The dusky eagle owl belongs to the family Strigidae. It is large, greyish-brown with prominent ear tufts, greyish-white underparts with dark streaks, and dark brown wings with whitish streaks. It is found in Pakistan, India, Nepal, Bangladesh, Myanmar, Thailand, Malaysia, and China. It inhabits thick riverside forests and old-growth trees.

questions.question: Drawida vazhania, that was recently seen in news, belongs to which species?
questions.explanation: Researchers discovered a new earthworm species Drawida vazhania in Peechi-Vazhani Wildlife Sanctuary. It was found near Vazhani Dam in Kerala. The species belongs to genus Drawida of the family Moniligastridae, mainly found in South, Southeast, and East Asia. It is a small-bodied earthworm belonging to the Barwelli species group. With this discovery, Drawida species recorded in India increased to 83. The Western Ghats now has 55 recorded Drawida species, highlighting its rich biodiversity.

questions.question: What is the IUCN conservation status of Hudsonian godwit that was recently seen in news?
questions.explanation: The Hudsonian godwit population has declined by 95% in the last four decades due to environmental changes. It is a large migratory shorebird belonging to the sandpiper family Scolopacidae. It breeds in sub-Arctic and Boreal wetland habitats like sedge meadows and muskeg. It is found mainly in North America and South America. It feeds on insects and crustaceans in shallow water. It migrates annually from the Arctic to Patagonia for the southern summer. Major threats include habitat loss and climate change. It is classified as Vulnerable by the International Union for Conservation of Nature Red List.

questions.question: The Indian softshell turtle is a freshwater turtle species native to which region?
questions.explanation: Police rescued 16 Indian Softshell turtles from illegal wildlife trade in Jharcha, Greater Noida, highlighting ongoing trafficking concerns. The Indian softshell turtle is a freshwater turtle species native to South Asia. They are found in rivers, canals, ponds, and freshwater systems. They are distributed across major river basins like Indus, Ganga, and Mahanadi. They occurs in multiple Indian states (e.g., Assam, Bihar, Uttar Pradesh, Odisha, West Bengal) and also in Afghanistan, Bangladesh, Nepal, and Pakistan.

questions.question: The Chambal River, that was recently seen in news, originates from which region?
questions.explanation: The Supreme Court of India stayed a Rajasthan government notification that had altered the boundaries of the National Chambal Gharial Sanctuary, which had freed 732 hectares along the Chambal River. The Chambal River is a perennial river originating from Janapav Hills, south of Mhow town near Indore, along the southern slopes of the Vindhya Range in Madhya Pradesh. It flows in a north-northeasterly direction through Madhya Pradesh, passes through Rajasthan briefly, forms part of the boundary between Rajasthan and Madhya Pradesh, and then turns southeast to join the Yamuna River in Uttar Pradesh.
